creator: freebsd-2024@virtual-estates.net
text:
At present the filenames of the patches downloaded directly from Github are of two kinds:

1. $commithash.diff (the majority).
2. $pullrequest.diff (textproc/pup, net/libnatpmp, deskutils/py-taskw).

Neither format provides a hint of what's inside the patch. Some ports describe such patches with comments inside Makefile, but browsing one's /usr/ports/distfiles you have no idea...

In addition, the second format is prone to filename-clashes -- because pull-requests from different Github projects may have the same numbers.

For example, I'd like to add 3 more patches to net-im/tdlib-purple (see Bug 278263): 129, 154, and 163. It is pure luck, that these do not conflict with the 168, that's already used by deskutils/py-taskw.

A possible work-around is for a port to set DIST_SUBDIR (such as to ${PORTNAME}). However, this will affect the location of both DISTFILES and PATCHFILES. So, unless the DIST_SUBDIR is set ahead of time, before the DISTFILES are downloaded, they will be duplicated after DIST_SUBDIR is added to house the patch(es) being added to the port.

The Mk/bsd.port.mk should either allow to fetch things under different names generically, or automagically take care of names for patches downloaded from Github (and, maybe, some other such sites). Another possibility may be to introduce PATCH_DIST_SUBDIR (defaulting it to ${DIST_SUBDIR}) to allow saving the patches separately from DISTFILES.

Personally, I'm not sure, which option should be implemented -- portmgr's collective wisdom ought to decide.
